bibliographic_data.title: Belief-Grounded Networks for Accelerated Robot Learning under Partial Observability
bibliographic_data.abstract: Many important robotics problems are partially observable where a single visual or force-feedback measurement is insufficient to reconstruct the state. Standard approaches involve learning a policy over beliefs or observation-action histories, but both have drawbacks; it is expensive to track the belief online, and it is hard to learn policies directly over histories. We propose a method for policy learning under partial observability called the Belief-Grounded Network (BGN) in which an auxiliary belief-reconstruction loss incentivizes a neural network to concisely summarize its input history. Since the resulting policy is a function of the history rather than the belief, it can be executed easily at runtime. We compare BGN against several baselines on classic benchmark tasks as well as three novel robotic force-feedback tasks. BGN outperforms all other tested methods and its learned policies work well when transferred onto a physical robot.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes the Belief-Grounded Network (BGN) to address policy learning under partial observability in robotics, incentivizing neural networks to summarize input history through an auxiliary belief-reconstruction loss.
core_content.key_methodology: The BGN method adds a belief-reconstruction loss to a deep reinforcement learning agent during simulated training, encouraging the network to generate features that summarize the history. This allows for policies based on histories rather than beliefs, making them easier to execute at runtime.
outcomes: BGN outperforms several baselines and even an agent that learns directly from true belief representations in both classic POMDP tasks and novel robotic force-feedback manipulation tasks.
The learned policies transfer well to a physical robot without adjustments.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
Belief-Grounded Networks for Accelerated Robot Learning under Partial Observability

Many important robotics problems are partially observable where a single visual or force-feedback measurement is insufficient to reconstruct the state. Standard approaches involve learning a policy over beliefs or observation-action histories, but both have drawbacks; it is expensive to track the belief online, and it is hard to learn policies directly over histories. We propose a method for policy learning under partial observability called the Belief-Grounded Network (BGN) in which an auxiliary belief-reconstruction loss incentivizes a neural network to concisely summarize its input history. Since the resulting policy is a function of the history rather than the belief, it can be executed easily at runtime. We compare BGN against several baselines on classic benchmark tasks as well as three novel robotic force-feedback tasks. BGN outperforms all other tested methods and its learned policies work well when transferred onto a physical robot.

The Belief-Grounded Network (BGN) is introduced to address the challenge of learning policies under partial observability in robotics. The method adds an auxiliary belief-reconstruction loss during simulated training, which incentivizes the neural network to generate features that summarize the history. This allows for policies based on histories rather than beliefs, making them easier to execute at runtime. BGN outperforms several strong baselines and even an agent that learns directly from true belief representations in both classic POMDP tasks and novel robotic force-feedback manipulation tasks. The learned policies transfer well to a physical robot without adjustments.
